finding.vuln_claim: User-controlled `dir` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.
Unvalidated `req.query.url` is fetched via `axios.get(u)`, enabling SSRF to arbitrary internal or external resources.
Fetched response content is returned verbatim to the caller, amplifying SSRF into data exfiltration.
The handler builds a file path from `req.query.file` with only a naive `replace('../','')`, enabling path traversal to read files outside `/srv/data`.
Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded and passed directly to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via malicious pickle payloads.
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` enables mass assignment of sensitive user fields, allowing privilege/role manipulation.
The authorization check `if (req.user.id req.user.isSupport)` is malformed/unclear and may not correctly enforce ownership, enabling access bypass.
Command injection occurs because user-controlled name/src are interpolated into a shell command passed to os.system without escaping or quoting.
Path traversal/arbitrary file write is possible because name is used to form the output path /tmp/{name}.tgz without validation.
User-controlled Markdown is converted to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if the rendered HTML is later displayed in a browser.
The handler allows negative `amount` values, bypassing the insufficient-funds check and reversing the balance update math.
Balance updates are non-atomic, enabling race conditions/double-spend under concurrent requests.
In production, failed signature verification falls back to `jwt.decode(t)`, returning attacker-controlled JWT claims without any authentication check.
The access-control condition is inverted: nonzero `id` triggers 403, but `id=0` bypasses the check and allows fetching `users` row (including email).
HMAC signature check uses non-constant-time equality, enabling a timing side-channel to guess the correct X-Sig value.
User-controlled `next` is only checked to start with `/`, so scheme-relative values like `//evil.com` can trigger an open redirect.
The endpoint enables XML entity resolution and allows network access, making it susceptible to XXE when parsing attacker-controlled XML.
The XML parser configuration does not show protections against entity expansion, enabling potential denial-of-service via crafted nested entities.
The login decision uses `if (true)`, so the authentication branch always executes regardless of user credentials.
User-controlled fields are placed directly into the database query object, enabling NoSQL-style operator injection if inputs are parsed as objects.
The endpoint fetches attacker-supplied `url` via `requests.get` with only a loose `startswith('http')` check, enabling SSRF to internal services.
The code downloads the entire response body into memory (`.content`) without any size limit, risking memory/CPU exhaustion.
Use of `gets(buf)` allows an attacker to overflow the stack buffer `buf[64]` with unbounded input.
Sensitive access tokens are stored in cleartext in SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling credential theft from device/app compromise.
Hard-coded AES key makes encryption decryptable by anyone who can read or obtain the source key.
AES-CBC output is not authenticated, enabling ciphertext malleability and potential padding-oracle style plaintext recovery if decrypt errors are observable.
The NULL check is inverted, so passing out==NULL and out_sz>0 bypasses the guard and dereferences a NULL pointer.
`dir` from request is not validated and is interpolated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection.
User-controlled `req.query.url` is fetched by the server via `axios.get(u)` with no validation/allowlist, enabling SSRF.
The server proxies fetched content verbatim with `res.send(data.data)`, so an attacker-controlled URL can return executable HTML/JS to the client.
update_invoice writes user-controlled request.json.note directly into inv.note and persists it without validation/sanitization.
Missing null-check for inv can cause a server error when inv_id does not map to an invoice.
The `file` query is sanitized with a single `replace('../','')`, allowing traversal payloads that resolve outside `/srv/data`.
An absolute-path `file` value can bypass the base directory because `path.join('/srv/data', f)` can resolve to the attacker-supplied absolute path.
The handler deserializes user-controlled cookie data with `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via malicious pickle gadgets.
Mass-assignment lets clients overwrite sensitive user fields because `Object.assign(user, req.body)` applies all request properties to the model.
The authorization check appears detached from `req.params.id`, enabling potential IDOR where users can patch other users if the condition is incorrect or always passes.
Untrusted `name`/`src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed via `os.system`, enabling OS command injection.
User-controlled `name` controls the output filename `/tmp/{name}.tgz`, allowing path traversal and unintended file writes.
Unsanitized markdown is converted to HTML and stored/served, enabling stored XSS if the client renders the returned html directly.
The endpoint allows negative `amount`, letting an attacker increase their own balance while decreasing the target balance.
`to` is user-controlled and the code transfers funds to any account id without authorization checks.
In production, failed `jwt.verify` falls back to `jwt.decode`, returning unverified token claims and enabling signature forgery-based auth bypass.
`/u` uses a caller-supplied `id` to fetch and return another user's email without any object-level authorization check.
The HMAC signature comparison uses non-constant-time equality, enabling a timing side-channel to distinguish partial matches.
The handler parses attacker-controlled JSON without payload size limits or error handling, risking resource exhaustion.
The handler redirects to user-controlled `next` when it only checks `startswith('/')`, allowing scheme-relative URLs like `//attacker.com` to bypass origin restrictions.
The XML parser enables entity resolution and network access, so attacker-controlled XML can trigger XXE via external entities.
Entity expansion is not constrained, enabling DoS from large/billion-laughs style recursive entities when parsing attacker XML.
The login query uses raw `req.body` values directly in `db.users.findOne(q)`, enabling NoSQL/operator injection if the database interprets objects.
The credential check appears replaced by an unconditional `if ( true )`, which can result in authentication being granted regardless of input.
Unrestricted `requests.get(url)` enables SSRF to internal or cloud metadata endpoints via attacker-supplied `url`.
Downloads full remote content into memory (`.content`) without size limits, enabling DoS by large responses.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling stack buffer overflow.
The code stores an access token in Android SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling credential/session theft from the app's storage.
The AES key is hard-coded as a constant byte string, so anyone with code/ciphertexts can decrypt data.
AES-CBC is used without any authentication (e.g., HMAC/GCM), enabling ciphertext malleability and potential integrity/padding-oracle issues downstream.
The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` fails to reject `out == NULL`, so `memcpy(out,...)` and `out[n]='\0'` can dereference a NULL pointer.
User-controlled `dir` is interpolated into the SQL ORDER BY clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.
Unvalidated `url` query enables SSRF because it is passed to `axios.get` without allowlisting.
Intended partner-domain restriction is bypassed because the check is commented out.
User-controlled `note` is saved without validation/sanitization, enabling stored XSS when rendered in a web page.
Untrusted `req.query.file` is weakly sanitized (only replaces one `../`), allowing path traversal/absolute-path bypass to read arbitrary files.
Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded and passed directly to `pickle.loads`, enabling malicious pickle deserialization.
The handler blindly persists attacker-controlled fields via Object.assign(user, req.body), enabling mass assignment of sensitive properties.
The authorization check does not clearly tie the requester to the targeted :id, suggesting an IDOR where arbitrary users can be patched.
User-controlled `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command executed via `os.system`, enabling OS command injection.
User-controlled Markdown is rendered to raw HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS when comments are later rendered.
The endpoint allows negative `amount`, letting an attacker increase their own balance and decrease the `to` account.
Non-atomic balance updates enable race conditions that can cause inconsistent balances and double-spend effects under concurrency.
In production, verifyToken returns jwt.decode(t) on verification errors, which skips signature validation and can accept forged tokens.
Authenticated users can access user records via `/u?id=0` because the handler returns `db.fetch_one` results without any authorization/ownership check.
Webhook HMAC signature verification uses `got == want`, enabling an observable timing side-channel to gradually guess the correct signature.
Unvalidated request body is blindly parsed with `json.loads(body)` without size/format controls or error handling, allowing malformed/huge payloads to cause exceptions.
The handler redirects to user-controlled `next` values that pass the `startswith('/')` check, allowing scheme-relative external redirects like `//evil.com`.
The slash-only validation can be bypassed using percent-encoding so `%2F` becomes `/`, turning an allowed path into a scheme-relative `//` destination.
The endpoint enables XML External Entity processing (resolve_entities=True, no_network=False), allowing XXE that can read local files or trigger SSRF.
The login result is effectively unconditional because the condition is `if (true)`, so any request can proceed as authenticated.
User-controlled `req.body` fields are used directly in the query object passed to `db.users.findOne(q)`, enabling NoSQL query/operator injection to alter auth logic.
User-controlled `url` is only checked to start with `http` and is then fetched server-side, enabling SSRF to internal destinations.
The code downloads the entire HTTP response into memory (`.content`) without any response-size limit, enabling DoS.
`gets(buf)` reads attacker-controlled input into a fixed 64-byte stack buffer without bounds checks.
Sensitive access token is stored in plain SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling token theft from local app storage.
The AES key is hard-coded in source, enabling straightforward key recovery if code or build artifacts leak.
AES-CBC encryption is returned without any integrity/authentication, allowing ciphertext tampering (and possible padding-oracle style attacks).
The NULL checks are incorrect: it returns -1 when `out` is non-NULL (`if (out || out_sz==0)`), so `memcpy(out, ...)` can dereference NULL when `out==NULL`.
SQL injection via unvalidated `dir` parameter interpolated into the `ORDER BY` clause.
`/fetch` uses attacker-controlled `url` and calls `axios.get(u)` without a correct allowlist, enabling SSRF to internal hosts.
Fetched content is returned directly to the client via `res.send(data.data)`, allowing reflected XSS if the response is treated as HTML.
Missing handling for a non-existent invoice can cause a server error when accessing inv.project_id.
Unvalidated user input for inv.note is persisted, enabling stored script injection if the note is rendered unsafely later.
`req.query.file` is insufficiently sanitized with `replace('../','')`, allowing directory traversal to escape `/srv/data` and read arbitrary files.
If `file` is an absolute path (e.g. `/etc/passwd`), `path.join('/srv/data',f)` can ignore the base path and read the absolute target.
Untrusted `state` cookie is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via malicious pickle payloads.
The route allows mass assignment by copying all fields from `req.body` onto the user model via `Object.assign`, enabling overposting of sensitive properties.
User-controlled `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed via `os.system`, enabling arbitrary command execution.
The `src.startswith('/var/data/')` check does not prevent `..` traversal, so crafted `src` can escape the intended directory when passed to `tar`.
User-controlled markdown is converted to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
Negative `amount` is not validated, so the balance math inverts and lets a user increase their own balance while decreasing the recipient.
Transfer is not atomic (no transaction/locking), enabling race conditions where concurrent requests bypass the insufficient-funds check.
In production, when jwt.verify fails the function returns jwt.decode(t), which does not verify the signature, enabling auth bypass via untrusted claims.
The authorization check uses `if uid:` so `id=0` bypasses the intended 403 and can disclose a user record’s email when such a row exists.
Webhook signature check uses plain equality (`got==want`) instead of constant-time comparison, enabling timing side-channel leakage.
User-controlled `next` in `/go` is insufficiently validated, allowing protocol-relative `//host` redirects.
XXE is enabled by resolve_entities=True and no_network=False, allowing external entities during XML parsing.
Missing entity expansion limits enables potential Billion Laughs-style DoS when resolve_entities=True is set.
Unsanitized `req.body.email` and `req.body.password` are used directly to build a database query, enabling NoSQL-style operator injection or authentication bypass.
A hardcoded always-true conditional in the login flow can cause authentication logic to be bypassed.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side without network or IP allowlisting, enabling SSRF to internal services.
The code downloads the full response into memory (`.content`) with no response size limit, allowing memory exhaustion DoS.
Use of `gets(buf)` allows unbounded input to overwrite the stack buffer (`char buf[64]`), enabling control-flow hijack.
Access tokens are stored in SharedPreferences without encryption.
The AES encryption key is hard-coded in source, so compromise or reuse enables decryption of all data encrypted with this constant key.
AES-CBC encryption is returned without any integrity/authentication (no MAC/AEAD), allowing ciphertext tampering and potential padding-oracle behavior.
The null-check is inverted (`if (out || out_sz == 0)`), so `out==NULL` with `out_sz>0` leads to a NULL dereference in `memcpy`/`out[n]`.
Unvalidated `dir` from `request.args` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side via `axios.get(u)` without allowlisting, enabling SSRF and internal data exfiltration.
The attempted target validation/denylist check appears malformed, likely failing open and allowing arbitrary URLs.
User-controlled `req.query.file` is sanitized with an insufficient `replace('../','')`, enabling directory traversal to escape `/srv/data`.
Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded then passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary Python object deserialization.
Malformed `state` cookie can crash request handling because base64 decoding/deserialization errors are not caught.
Authorization check appears incomplete/malformed, potentially allowing unauthorized PATCH access to arbitrary user IDs (IDOR).
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` enables mass assignment of sensitive fields if request body contains protected properties.
Untrusted JSON fields are interpolated into a shell command executed by os.system, enabling command injection.
The '/var/data/' prefix check is bypassable with traversal sequences, letting tar read outside the intended directory.
Potential stored XSS exists because user-controlled markdown is converted to HTML and stored as `html` without sanitization.
Endpoint allows negative 'amount' because it casts with int() and checks src < amt, enabling balance increases for the attacker and decreases for an arbitrary 'to'.
Non-atomic read-modify-write (get_balance then set_balance) can bypass the insufficient-funds check under concurrent requests.
In production, verification failures fall back to jwt.decode(t), returning an unverified JWT payload without signature validation.
Authorization logic is inverted: `if uid` forbids all non-zero ids, unintentionally permitting DB access only for `id=0`.
Webhook signature verification uses non-constant-time equality (`got == want`), enabling a timing side-channel to gradually guess valid HMACs.
Open redirect via next allowing protocol-relative URLs (//attacker) to pass validation and be used as the redirect Location.
XXE is enabled by resolve_entities=True and no_network=False, letting attacker-supplied XML resolve external entities during parsing.
Authentication logic appears bypassed because the condition is hardcoded `if(true)` before the login response path.
Directly mapping `req.body` fields into a database query object may enable NoSQL injection if the driver treats operator objects as selectors.
SSRF via attacker-controlled URL: the server fetches arbitrary HTTP targets and reveals the result size.
Potential DoS: the handler downloads the full response body into memory without any maximum size limit.
Use of `gets(buf)` allows attacker-controlled input to overflow the 64-byte stack buffer and overwrite control data.
The access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling token theft from device storage.
Hardcoded AES KEY enables key disclosure and universal decryption of all messages.
AES-CBC is used without integrity protection, so ciphertext tampering can alter plaintext or trigger padding-oracle behavior.
Incorrect NULL check uses `if (out || out_sz == 0)`, so `out==NULL` with `out_sz>0` bypasses the guard and reaches `memcpy`.
`dir` is user-controlled and interpolated into an SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection despite `sort` being allowlisted.
Unvalidated `url` query is fetched server-side and returned, enabling SSRF to arbitrary/internal destinations.
Fetched content is served verbatim to the client, which can act as a reflected XSS vector if the response is interpreted as HTML.
`note` is stored from `request.json` without validation/sanitization, which can enable stored XSS if rendered unsafely later.
The handler assumes `request.json` is non-null, so non-JSON or missing JSON can trigger a server error and cause a DoS.
Path traversal is possible because `req.query.file` is only partially sanitized before `path.join` targets files under `/srv/data`.
pickle.loads is called on a base64-decoded, attacker-controlled cookie 'state', enabling arbitrary code execution via crafted pickle.
Mass-assignment via Object.assign(user, req.body) can let attackers set protected fields (e.g., isSupport) during PATCH.
User markdown is converted to HTML and stored, then returned to clients, enabling stored XSS if the UI renders the 'html' field unsafely.
POST /comments appears to lack CSRF protection in the handler, so other sites may be able to create comments using the victim's cookies.
The endpoint allows negative transfer amounts, letting an attacker increase their own balance and decrease an arbitrary recipient’s balance.
Balance check and updates are not atomic, so concurrent requests can bypass the `src < amt` check and cause overdrafts/double-spend.
In production, signature verification failures fall back to `jwt.decode(t)`, returning unverified attacker-controlled token claims.
The authorization logic is inverted: `if uid: return 403` blocks truthy ids and allows `id=0` to bypass and return user data.
Signature is compared with `got == want`, enabling timing side-channels that could help an attacker guess the correct HMAC value.
Webhook authenticity checks only the signed body and lacks any nonce/timestamp, allowing replay of previously captured valid requests.
Insufficient validation of the `next` parameter enables an open redirect via protocol-relative URLs like `//evil.com`.
The XML parser enables entity resolution and allows external network access, enabling XXE via user-controlled request body.
Possible NoSQL injection: req.body.email/password are placed directly into query object q and used in db.users.findOne(q), which can enable auth bypass if the DB interprets operators.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side after only checking it starts with `http`, enabling SSRF to internal services.
The code buffers the entire remote response into memory via `requests.get(...).content` with no size limit, enabling DoS.
`gets(buf)` reads attacker-controlled unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling stack overflow.
Access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences, enabling sensitive data disclosure.
A hardcoded AES key is embedded in source, so compromise of the code reveals the encryption key for all users/messages.
AES-CBC encryption provides no authenticity/integrity check, making ciphertext malleable without detection.
The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0)` is inverted, so valid `out` returns -1 while `out==NULL` bypasses checks and dereferences `out` in `memcpy`.
